title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Credit Prices Drop Alongside Fall in Transfer Volumes Week Over Week
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights

(27th October â€“ 2ndÂ November 2025)
  • A total of 384,539 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a rise of 9.4 % week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a notable decrease of 62.53% WoW as 1,077,237 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $54.08/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 1.06% fall WoW; prices of all non-zero transfers saw a fall of 6.26%, priced at $55.57/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price rose by 0.40% WoW; on 5th November, the spot price stood at $52.88/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $53.50/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices fell by 2.07% WoW. D4 prices rose by 1.02% WoW , while D6 prices fell by 2.97% WoW; D5 prices remained unchanged WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

Last week, the Renewable Fuels Association (RFA) submitted comments to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) on its supplemental proposal concerning the reallocation of biofuel blending volumes lost to small refinery exemptions under the Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS). The RFA commended the EPA for restoring expired renewable identification numbers (RINs) to refiners with exemptions from 2016â€“2022, helping maintain market integrity. However, it urged the agency to fully reallocate all exempted volumes for 2023â€“2025 to meet statutory blending targets. In California, the LCFS quarterly reports revealed a rise in credit generation in Q2 2025 to 9.66 MMT, led by strong gains in Bio-CNG (up 33.99% QoQ), Renewable Diesel (up 14.43% QoQ), and EV charging (up 4.78% QoQ), while SAF credits fell 26.39% QoQ. Overall deficits climbed 14.39% QoQ to 6.49 MMT, driven by higher Diesel (up 18.51% YoY) and Fossil CNG (up 87.66% YoY) volumes.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_607034" align="alignnone" width="613"] Table 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 transfer prices recorded a 1.06% WoW decrease, falling from $54.66 to $54.08 this week, and all non-zero transfer prices fell from $59.28 to $55.57, recording a fall of 6.26%. Type 1 trades witnessed 384,539 credits exchanged, recording a 9.46% rise WoW. The total transaction volumes for all non-zero trades saw a decrease of 62.53% WoW, reaching 1,077,237 credits. [caption id="attachment_607035" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_607036" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices rose by 0.40% WoW, reaching $54.78/MT. On 5th November, the spot price stood at $52.88/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $53.50/MT on 5th November.

LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_607037" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 49 trades were reported in the market, with 9 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $61.00 for a Type 2 transaction at a volume of 2,500 credits. The lowest transfer by price was $46.25 per credit for a Type 2 transaction reported for 75,000 credits. The largest volume of transfers involved 300,000 credits for a Type 2 transaction priced at $60.35. The smallest volume of transfers involved 25 credits for a Type 1 transaction priced at $53.50.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_607038" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average spot Type 1 price stood at $53.80 in October so far, falling from $53.94 in September. Trade volumes for the month of October stood at 13.10M as of 5th November, 2025. This month has witnessed the highest monthly transaction volumes ever in the history of the program.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_607039" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 27th Octoberâ€“5th November 2025, total VPT fell by 5.75% WoW, with a fall in all non-zero credit prices for the last week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices fell by 2.07% WoW. D4 prices rose by 1.02% WoW, while D6 prices fell by 2.97% WoW; D5 prices remained unchanged WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming DatesÂ 

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments This Week

  • Thompson, Neal, Ways & Means Democrats Introduce Bill to Restore Historic Renewable Energy Investments: Ranking Member of the House Ways and Means Subcommittee on Tax Policy Mike Thompson (CA-04), Ranking Member of the House Ways and Means Committee Richard E. Neal (MA-01), and the Democratic membership of the Ways & Means Committee announced the introduction of H.R. 5862, the Energy Independence and Affordability Act (EIAA). The EIAA would restore Rep. Thompsonâ€™s historic renewable energy tax incentives passed in the 2022 Inflation Reduction Act. â€œWe applaud Congressman Mike Thompson for his leadership on the American Clean Energy Independence and Affordability Act,â€ said the League of Conservation Voters Vice President of Federal Policy Matthew Davis.
  • Budzinski Calls on EPA to Support Homegrown Biofuels Ahead of Final Renewable Fuel Standard Rule: Congresswoman Nikki Budzinski (IL-13) led 48 bipartisan Members of Congress in a letter to U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Administrator Lee Zeldin expressing support for the EPAâ€™s proposed Renewable Volume Obligations (RVOs) under the Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS) â€œSet 2â€ rule. In the letter, the bipartisan and bicameral group of lawmakers also shared their support for the reallocation of 100% of the waived gallons from any granted Small Refinery Exemptions (SREs) and urged the EPA to finalize the rule as quickly as possible. â€œAt a time when farm families and rural communities face tough markets and economic uncertainty, we appreciate EPAâ€™s recognition of the vital role American biofuels and agriculture play in lowering fuel prices and advancing American energy dominance,â€ the lawmakers wrote.
  • Clean Fuel Regulations Quarterly Compliance Credit Market Report Q1 2025: Environment and Climate Change Canada (ECCC) has published a credit market report for quarter 1 of the 2025 compliance period. This report, the Clean Fuel Regulations 2025 Quarterly Credit Market Report - Q1, presents data on the compliance credit market, summarizing data on quarterly credit creation between January 1, 2025 to March 31, 2025 (only representing credits created on a quarterly basis), and data on credit transfers for transactions completed for the 12-month period leading to the end of Q1 2025 (i.e. from April 1st, 2024, to March 31st, 2025). Compliance with the 2024 compliance period is still ongoing, with the submission of compliance reports submitted on July 31, 2025 and the Compliance-Credit Clearance Mechanism (CCM) ending October 31, 2025 for 15,302 credits at a price cap of $319. This report, the Clean Fuel Regulations 2025 Quarterly Credit Market Report - Q1, presents data on the compliance credit market, summarizing data on quarterly credit creation between January 1, 2025 to March 31, 2025 (only representing credits created on a quarterly basis), and data on credit transfers for transactions completed for the 12-month period leading to the end of Q1 2025 (i.e. from April 1st, 2024, to March 31st, 2025.
  • Oregon DEQ to Suspend EV Charge Ahead Rebate on Friday, Dec. 5: The Oregon Department of Environmental Quality announced that it will suspend the Charge Ahead Rebate for new and used electric vehicles on Friday, Dec. 5, 2025, due to high demand and projections for distributing remaining 2025 funds. Those wishing to qualify for this specific rebate must purchase or lease a new or used, eligible electric vehicle by 11:59 p.m. (PST) on Thursday, Dec. 4, 2025.
  • RFA Calls for Full Reallocation of 2023-2025 SREs, Supports Return of Expired RINs for 2016-2022 SREs: The Renewable Fuels Association submitted comments to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency in response to its supplemental proposal addressing reallocation of biofuel blending volumes lost to small refinery exemptions under the Renewable Fuel Standard. RFA praised EPA for taking steps to protect market integrity by returning expired renewable identification numbers (RINs) to refiners that were granted exemptions for 2016-2022, years for which compliance is now closed. For the 2023-2025 compliance years, however, the association strongly urged EPA to reallocate 100 percent of exempted renewable fuel volumes, given the agencyâ€™s statutory obligation to ensure that finalized blending requirements are met. Additionally, RFA reiterated its position that EPA must prospectively reallocate exempted volumes anticipated for 2026 and 2027.
  • Clean Fuels Urges EPA to Fully Reallocate Exempted RFS Volumes: Clean Fuels Alliance America commended EPA for taking action to ensure that robust 2026 and 2027 Renewable Fuel Standards are not eroded by small refinery exemptions. Clean Fuels urged the agency to adopt supplemental â€œSRE reallocation volumesâ€ and updated estimates of exempted volumes for 2026 and 2027 representing 100% of all granted, pending, and expected exemptions for the 2023 â€“ 2025 compliance years. Clean Fuels also asked the agency to be judicious in granting exemptions.
